subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Uncertainty propagation equations are correctly applied and clearly presented.
Graphs are well-labelled and trend lines aid interpretation.
weaknesses: Data tables contain major typos and inconsistencies (e.g., unit errors, redundant columns).
Uncertainties are incompletely treated: no uncertainty on gradient or percentage uncertainties, and missing error bars.
Processed data have inaccuracies (constant ÎM repeated) and mis-used angle error.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While there is evidence of some correct processing and clear graph labelling, significant omissions and inaccuracies dominate (band 1â2). A provisional score of 1 reflects the limited precision and many major flaws in data recording, processing and uncertainty treatment.

strengths: The research question is situated in a specific, well-defined context (detailed track setup, mass increments, Go Direct cart).
Methodology is described step-by-step with instrument models, uncertainties and control measures, enabling reproducibility.
Instrumentation tables (Tables 1â3) are thorough, specifying measurement methods and precisions. â
weaknesses: Literature integration remains descriptive; specific findings from cited studies are not critically used to justify design choices.
The assumption of negligible friction is untested; no preliminary measurements of Î¼ are provided.
Sampling sufficiency (four data points per phase) and timing of mass changes lack full justification or flowchart clarification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores (5, 5, 3) average ~4.3, showing very strong context and reproducible methodology but limited critical integration and explanation of some choices. A band-3â4 award of 4 reflects these strengths while recognizing missing justifications that prevent the maximal 5â6.

strengths: Qualitative trends (constant velocity for mass loss, decrease for mass gain) are correctly identified.
Application of basic inelasticâcollision momentum equation links the results to theory.
weaknesses: No quantitative comparison between observed slopes and theoretical predictions.
Residual friction is not quantified or incorporated into the conclusion.
Conclusion remains largely qualitative with sparse analytical support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion shows some consistency with analysis (band 3â4) but lacks quantitative testing against theory and neglects friction effects. Given the partial support and limited depth, a minimal award of 1 is appropriate under the total-mark constraint.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (friction, angle uncertainty, mass distribution) are identified.
Realistic improvements (lower-friction track, automated release) are suggested.
weaknesses: Relative impacts of errors are not quantified or ranked.
No statistical measures (standard deviation, standard error) are provided to support claims about precision.
Improvements are stated but not explained in terms of expected gains in accuracy.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student names relevant limitations and proposes realistic improvements (band 3â4), the lack of quantification of error impacts and explanation of how suggestions improve results limits the evaluation to a basic level. A score of 1 reflects these constraints while aligning with the overall quality and ensuring the total sums to 7.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student situates the research question in a clearly defined experimental situation (a 1.75Â° low-friction track, specific masses, Go Direct cart) and explains why the investigation matters for variable-mass systems in mechanics and engineering. This goes well beyond a broad mention and provides a **specific, appropriate context** that allows a reader to see the practical relevance. Minor stylistic flaws do not obscure the precision of the context, so the work fits comfortably in the 5â6 band; a top-band mark of 5 is awarded because, while specific, the link to previously published research is descriptive rather than critically integrated.

Instrument uncertainties are stated and simple propagation for ÎM and Îv is performed, with error bars shown on graphs. Nevertheless, there is **no treatment of percentage uncertainty, no uncertainty on gradient, and the Â±0.5Â° angle error is mis-used**. These omissions place the work at âevidence with some significant omissionsâ, warranting a score of 3 in the 3â4 band.

Variables, controls and uncertainty sources are **described**, but the student does not fully justify how the chosen four data points per phase are sufficient to answer the question, nor is there discussion of sampling rate or statistical treatment. The considerations move beyond simple listing (e.g., propagation formulae used), yet they fall short of an **explanation** of sufficiency or optimisation. Therefore the strand aligns with the mid-band (3â4); a conservative score of 3 is assigned.

Specific weaknesses such as residual friction, angle uncertainty and mass distribution are identified and their likely impact briefly described. The explanation of **relative impact** is limited (no magnitude estimates), so the strand is best placed at the top of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 is therefore assigned.

The methodology is laid out step-by-step (angle set, sensor configured, mass added/removed, repeated three times, data exported). Instruments, uncertainties and control measures are tabulated. A peer could **replicate the experiment with little or no additional information**. Although some formatting is cluttered, essential details such as sensor model, resolution, mass increments and release method are present, satisfying the descriptor for full reproducibility. A score of 5 is appropriate because the explanation of why each design choice was made is limited, preventing award of the maximal 6.

Only simple averages and linear fits are calculated; the dataset is extremely small (four points per condition) and some processed values (e.g., constant Îm and Îv columns) are plainly wrong. These issues constitute **major omissions/inaccuracies**, aligning with the 1â2 band. Because some correct processing (propagation, best-fit line) exists, the upper value of 2 is awarded.

For each stated limitation, the student proposes plausible improvements (e.g., lower-friction track, automated release). These are **relevant and realistic but not fully explained** in terms of how much they would improve accuracy/precision. This aligns with the 3â4 band; a mark of 3 is appropriate.

Data tables, units and headings are present and graphs are labelled; however, tables contain redundant columns, some symbols are inconsistent and significant figures are not applied consistently. Thus communication is **clear** enough to follow but not uniformly precise, matching the 3â4 band. A mark of 3 reflects the noticeable but non-fatal imprecision.

The student compares findings to the ideal plane formula and invokes momentum conservation literature, showing **some relevant comparison** with accepted context. The discussion is qualitative and lacks deeper quantitative reconciliation (e.g., predicted vs observed slopes), so it fits the mid-band with a mark of 3.

The conclusion restates the key trend (velocity constant for mass loss, decreases for mass gain) and links this qualitatively to inelastic-collision theory, which is broadly consistent with the presented analysis. Still, the reasoning relies on sparse data and does not quantitatively test theoretical predictions, so the support is **partial rather than full**. This merits the 3â4 band; a score of 3 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The assumption of negligible friction is stated but not empirically supported. Include preliminary friction measurements (e.g., coefficient Î¼) or tests to validate this assumption.
The research question (mass increments, angle, Go Direct cart) is clearly stated with precise parameters, enabling a reader to understand the scope and reproduce the experiment.
The hypothesis refers to non-ideal effects but lacks quantification. Estimate the expected change in velocity (Îv) using your inelastic-collision model to make the hypothesis more precise.
The predictive graph (Figure 1) lacks axis units and uncertainty bands. Annotate axes with units and include error margins to clarify expected data variability.
The instrumentation list is thorough, including models and uncertainties, which strengthens the methodological description and supports reproducibility.
The introduction situates the investigation within classical mechanics and variable-mass applications, providing a specific and relevant context that demonstrates practical significance.

While the context is detailed, the literature review remains descriptive. Critically integrate specific findings from Irschik & Holl (2004) or Pendrill et al. (2014) to justify experimental design choices.
Table 1 clearly defines independent variable mass, measurement method, and instrument precision, supporting reproducibility of data collection.
The dependent variable table (Table 2) provides a thorough description of velocity measurement equipment and connectivity, ensuring clarity in data recording methods.
The controls listed in Table 3 are comprehensive. To quantify control rigor, specify tolerance limits (e.g., Â±0.1Â° for angle) and environmental condition ranges.
Figure 2 schematic shows the setup but omits key dimensions and sensor positions. Label start/end points, mass-release mechanism, and sensor location for better reproducibility.
Raw data tables exclude measurement uncertainties for initial and final velocities. Include Îu and Îv values to support subsequent uncertainty analysis.
Table 5 header shows âÎ¸ =1.75 %â indicating a unit typo. Correct to â1.75 Â°â to avoid confusion in data interpretation.
Processed data table repeats a constant ÎM of 0.01400 kg rather than reflecting trial-specific values. Verify your propagation formula application to ensure accuracy.
The listed ÎÎ¸ uncertainty remains at 0.5 Â° for all trials despite repeated verifications. Calculate actual variation from multiple angle measurements rather than a fixed value.
Methodology steps describe phases but omit exact timing of mass extraction relative to sensor data capture. Include precise timing or trigger conditions for dynamic mass changes.
Provide a flowchart or timeline illustrating the two-phase procedure to clarify the sequence of adding and removing mass and data recording.
Consider reporting percentage uncertainties for mass and velocity to contextualize the size of error bars relative to measured values.
Equations for mass and velocity uncertainty propagation are correctly applied and clearly presented, demonstrating appropriate data-processing quality.
Velocity uncertainty propagation assumes independence of u and v measurements. Discuss potential correlation or justify this assumption to strengthen uncertainty analysis.
Graphs are well-labelled and show clear trends, aiding interpretation of the mass versus velocity relationship.
Graphs lack error bars or uncertainty shading. Include visual error representations to reflect measurement precision on each data point.
Application of the inelastic collision momentum equation $$m_i v_i = (m_i + \Delta m) v_f$$ effectively explains the decreasing velocity trend when mass is added.
Discussion omits quantification of residual friction. Integrate a friction term in the momentum equation or estimate its magnitude to explain any additional velocity reduction.

The conclusion restates qualitative trends but omits quantitative comparison between observed slope (-0.56 m/s per kg) and theoretical expectation from $$v_f=(m_i/(m_i+\Delta m))v_i$$. Include this to justify your conclusion.
Table 7 identifies systematic errors with realistic improvements, demonstrating a solid understanding of methodological weaknesses.
The impact of each limitation is described but not quantified. Estimate or rank the relative magnitude of errors (e.g., friction vs angle uncertainty) to explain their comparative importance.
For random errors, report statistical measures (e.g., standard deviation or standard error) in addition to descriptive text to quantify data precision.
Reference list shows inconsistent formatting (some missing DOIs and varied punctuation). Apply a uniform citation style (e.g., APA) for academic rigor.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

up a slope (175cm), using video analysis software (Tracker)?
